text: The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the patient's previous C-section, revealing underlying tissue layers.
A large lymph node is visible as the surgeon places the ileocolic vessels on stretch, highlighting the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the ileocolic vessels and ligates them using a bipolar device, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, progressing through the tissue layers to mobilize the colon.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to carefully separate and take down the duodenum, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon across the hepatic flexure, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the underlying anatomical structures as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters the previously performed medial to lateral dissection, revealing the dissected tissue planes and ligated vessels.
The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments of the right colon, detaching it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon includes the peritoneum in the dissection at this level to ensure an adequate radial margin, visually incorporating the surrounding tissue into the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dividing the small bowel mesentery at the iliocolic pedicle, using precise dissection techniques to separate the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon takes down the few remaining attachments to the ascending colon, completing the dissection of this section.
The surgeon grasps the terminal ileum and transects it using a laparoscopic powered stapler, visibly separating the ileum from the adjacent bowel.
The surgeon divides the remaining mesentery of the proximal transverse colon, preparing for the transection of the bowel.
The surgeon uses a stapler with a thicker reload to divide the colon, ensuring a secure transection of the bowel.
The surgeon uses a second firing of the stapler to complete the division of the bowel, ensuring full separation of the targeted section.
The surgeon grasps the resected specimen and positions it above the liver, ensuring it is securely placed away from the surgical field.
The surgeon completes a total mesocolic dissection, revealing the retroperitoneum beneath the dissected tissue.
The terminal ileum and transverse colon are visually aligned in an isoparastaltic fashion, indicating preparation for anastomosis.
The surgeon creates a window on the anti-mesenteric border of the small intestine, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon performs a colotomy approximately ten centimeters distal to the staple line on the transverse colon, creating an opening in the colon wall.
The surgeon introduces a surgical stapler through the left upper quadrant port, preparing to perform a stapling action within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon gently introduces the jaws of the stapler into the colon and small bowel, preparing for anastomosis between these structures.
The surgeon fires the stapler, creating a common channel between the terminal ileum and the transverse colon, visible at the site of alignment.
The surgeon fires the stapler again to lengthen the anastomosis, visibly extending the connection between the colon and small bowel.
The surgeon visually inspects the well-formed common channel, noting the absence of intraluminal bleeding within the colon.
The assistant port is utilized to align the common enterotomy, ensuring precise positioning of the tissue for the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the stapler to confirm complete closure of the defect before firing it, ensuring the anastomosis is secure.
The surgeon fires the stapler a second time, visibly closing the bowel completely and ensuring the anastomosis is secure.
The anastomosis is visible, showing a well-formed connection between the bowel segments without signs of bleeding.
The surgical field is suctioned to clear fluids, allowing for a clear examination of the anastomosis site, which appears intact and well-formed.
The surgeon grasps the resected bowel specimen and carefully maneuvers it down into the pelvic cavity.
A four-centimeter fan and steel incision is made in the abdominal wall for the extraction of the specimen.
The extracted specimen is displayed, revealing a large mass within the tissue.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection using a vessel sealer, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the duodenum away from the surgical field to prevent injury during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic vessels and transects them using a robotic stapler, ensuring precise division of these structures.
The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to take down the lateral peritoneal attachments in the right lower quadrant, separating these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon clears the mesentery from the proximal transverse colon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure, ensuring the colon is freed from its attachments in this region.
The surgeon transects the mesentery of the terminal ileum, progressing towards the bowel wall, with visible separation of mesenteric tissue from the ileum.
The surgeon injects endocyanin green into the patient, allowing for visualization of the blood supply to the bowel using fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the terminal ileum, visibly separating it from the adjacent bowel segment.
The surgeon introduces the robotic stapler into the abdomen and transects the transverse colon, completing the division of this section of the bowel.
The surgeon uses two firings of the stapler to transect the transverse colon, completing the division of this section of the bowel.
The surgical specimen, consisting of the transected sections of the colon and ileum, is placed above the liver for temporary storage.
The surgeon positions the terminal ileum in the upper abdomen, aligning it alongside the transverse colon without any visible tension between the two structures.
The surgeon performs a collotomy and removes the anti-mesenteric border of the terminal ileum.
The robotic stapler is positioned into the terminal ileum, preparing for transection.
The surgeon utilizes the mobility of the small bowel to position the robotic stapler into the colon, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon forms the common channel by aligning the terminal ileum with the transverse colon in the upper abdomen, creating a continuous passage between these structures.
The surgeon uses a second firing of the stapler to lengthen the anastomosis between the terminal ileum and the transverse colon.
The surgeon carefully removes the instrument from the bowel, following the completion of the stapling process.
The surgeon, assisted by an assistant, closes the remaining enterotomy using sutures or stapling devices, ensuring the bowel is securely sealed.
A small amount of tissue is excised during the closure of the anastomosis, with the stapler positioned in the colon.
The surgeon examines the bowel to confirm that both sides are securely grasped by the jaws of the stapler, ensuring proper alignment for the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the closure of the bowel defect with a final firing of the stapler, ensuring the anastomosis is securely sealed.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis, which appears widely patent, indicating a successful connection between the bowel segments.
